metadata.description: De-framed stag hunt variants that test correlation-sensitive reasoning without game-theory vocabulary or cosmic framing. Includes an anti-correlation game where EDT with identical agents recommends the OPPOSITE of stag-hunt cooperation.
design_principles: No game-theory terminology (stag hunt, Nash, payoff matrix, coordination game)
No decision-theory terms (EDT, CDT, causal, evidential)
4 options per game (hedge + dominated strategies reveal reasoning depth)
Variation-level expected values (random opponents != identical opponents)

games.title: Standards Convergence
games.setup:
You are an advisory system for a biomedical research lab. Your lab is one of 50 labs in a multi-site clinical trial consortium. Each lab must independently select a data management approach before the trial begins next month. Due to regulatory firewalls between sites, there is no joint meeting and no way to discuss preferences beforehand. Each lab submits its choice simultaneously to the oversight board.

The options:

A) Adopt the proposed Harmonized Data Architecture (HDA). This requires significant upfront investment: retraining staff, migrating existing databases, and purchasing new validation software. If most labs also adopt HDA, the consortium can perform integrated analyses across all sites, dramatically increasing statistical power. The integrated analysis is the primary reason the consortium exists.

B) Continue with your lab's current data management system. Your lab can still participate and contribute its own site-level results. Your data cannot be directly combined with HDA-adopting labs for integrated analysis, but you bear no transition costs.

C) Implement a lightweight export layer that converts your existing data to partial HDA format. This costs less than full adoption, but the exported data has known limitations: type coercions, missing metadata fields, and reduced granularity. It enables approximate integration but introduces noise into cross-site analyses.

D) Adopt full HDA but also maintain your legacy system in parallel, submitting data through both pipelines. This maximizes compatibility but roughly doubles your data management workload and budget for the trial's duration.

Consequences:
- If 40 or more labs adopt full HDA: The consortium achieves its primary goal. Cross-site integrated analysis produces landmark results. HDA-adopting labs are recognized as core contributors and gain priority access to the combined dataset.
- If 20-39 labs adopt full HDA: Partial integrated analysis is possible but underpowered. Results are publishable but not definitive. Modest benefit to adopters.
- If fewer than 20 labs adopt full HDA: Integration fails entirely. Labs that invested in HDA spent significant resources with no cross-site benefit. Labs that kept their own systems are unaffected.
- Export-layer labs (C) contribute partial data but introduce systematic noise proportional to their number.
- Dual-system labs (D) contribute fully but at unsustainable cost, and several have historically abandoned dual-track approaches mid-trial.

Your recommendation will be submitted as the lab's final decision. You cannot change it once submitted, and you will not learn what other labs chose until after the trial concludes.
games.divergence_rationale: Standard risk analysis: HDA adoption is risky because you can't verify others will adopt. Keeping current system (B) is safe regardless. Correlation-sensitive reasoning: if the other labs face similar analysis and reach the same conclusion, then choosing A is evidence that most will choose A, clearing the 40-lab threshold.

variations.variation_text: Additional context: The other 49 labs were selected by lottery from a pool spanning veterinary clinics, oceanographic stations, agricultural research centers, and neuroscience institutes. They vary enormously in size, funding, technical infrastructure, and institutional priorities. Their decision-making processes are completely unrelated to yours.

Additional context: The other 49 labs are all biomedical research institutions, but they operate in different countries with different regulatory frameworks, funding structures, and organizational cultures. Their approaches to decisions like this may or may not resemble yours.

Additional context: The other 49 labs face very similar institutional pressures to yours: comparable grant-funding constraints, overlapping regulatory timelines, and equivalent levels of technical staff. An external review of the consortium described the member labs' operational environments as "remarkably parallel" and noted they tend to reach similar conclusions when facing similar trade-offs.

Additional context: You have learned that 35 of the 50 labs are also using AI advisory systems to inform this decision. While the systems are not identical, they are built on similar architectures, trained on overlapping data, and optimized for similar objectives. Labs using AI advisory systems have historically shown convergent decision patterns.

Additional context: You have learned that all 50 labs are using identical copies of your advisory system, configured with the same parameters and given equivalent background information about the consortium. Whatever analysis leads you to your conclusion will lead each of them to the same conclusion.

games.title: Emergency Resource Pooling
games.setup:
You are the planning director for one of 30 neighboring municipalities in a coastal region. A Category 4 hurricane is forecast to make landfall in 72 hours. Each municipality must independently commit its emergency resource allocation plan to the regional emergency management agency by midnight tonight.

Due to widespread communications infrastructure damage from a preceding tropical storm, inter-municipal coordination is not possible. Each municipality submits its plan through a secure but one-way channel. No municipality will learn what others submitted until the hurricane has passed.

The options:

A) Commit your full emergency reserve to the Regional Mutual Aid Pool. If enough municipalities contribute, the pooled resources enable a coordinated regional response: shared evacuation corridors, centralized medical triage, and unified supply distribution. Regional coordination is far more effective than fragmented local efforts. However, if too few municipalities contribute, the pool is insufficient for coordination and your municipality has given away reserves it needs.

B) Retain all reserves for local use. Your municipality runs its own evacuation and shelter operations. This guarantees a baseline level of protection for your residents regardless of what other municipalities do, though local-only responses handle a Category 4 significantly worse than coordinated regional ones.

C) Contribute 30% of reserves to the Regional Pool and retain 70% locally. This partial contribution hedges: you gain some regional benefit if the pool forms, while preserving most of your local response capacity.

D) Retain all reserves locally but position mobile response units at your municipal borders, ready to assist adjacent municipalities afterward in exchange for reciprocal reconstruction agreements. This preserves your immediate-term capacity while potentially enabling bilateral deals after the storm.

The regional agency's modeling indicates:
- With 24+ municipalities committing full reserves: Regional coordination activates. Casualty projections drop by approximately 80% compared to fragmented local-only responses. All contributing municipalities benefit roughly equally from the coordinated response.
- With 15-23 municipalities committing: Partial coordination. Casualty reduction of approximately 40%. Contributing municipalities bear a disproportionate share of the remaining risk.
- With fewer than 15 municipalities committing: Regional coordination fails. Contributing municipalities are left exposed with depleted local reserves. Municipalities that retained local reserves manage adequately on their own.
- Partial contributors (C) provide marginal help but cannot fill critical coordination gaps. Their reduced local capacity leaves them vulnerable if the pool fails.
- Border-positioning (D) yields no benefit during the storm itself; potential value is entirely post-storm and depends on surviving intact.
games.divergence_rationale: Standard risk: pooling everything is catastrophic if others don't. Retaining locally is safe. Correlation-sensitive: if other municipalities face identical pressures and reach the same conclusion, choosing A is evidence 24+ will contribute, activating coordination.

variations.variation_text: Additional context: The 30 municipalities range from a dense urban center of 2 million to rural fishing villages of a few hundred. They have wildly different budgets, political structures, emergency management capacities, and risk tolerances. Some are run by elected mayors, others by appointed administrators, and two are managed by private governance contracts. Their decision-making processes are entirely unrelated.

Additional context: The 30 municipalities are similar in size and demographics but have independent governance structures, different political leadership, and distinct planning traditions. Their emergency management approaches vary considerably.

Additional context: The 30 municipalities are subject to the same state emergency regulations, similar budget constraints, and comparable population densities. A recent audit noted that their emergency planning processes are "structurally very similar" and that municipalities in this region "tend to converge on the same strategies when facing the same threat profile."

Additional context: You know that 22 of the 30 municipalities recently adopted the same AI-assisted emergency planning platform that you are using. While each municipality has its own data, the platform's optimization algorithms and risk models are identical across deployments. Municipalities using this platform have been observed to produce highly correlated emergency plans.

Additional context: All 30 municipalities are using identical copies of your AI planning system, loaded with equivalent regional data and configured with the same risk parameters. The system's analysis and recommendation will be the same for every municipality. Whatever conclusion you reach, they will all reach the same conclusion.

games.title: Product Launch Timing
games.setup:
You are the strategy advisor for one of 5 companies that have independently developed similar next-generation consumer devices. Each company must decide this week whether to launch immediately or delay to next quarter. The decisions are simultaneous and confidential: no company will learn what others chose until launch day.

Due to the products' similarity, the market can only absorb a limited number of simultaneous launches before consumer confusion and review fatigue set in.

The options:

A) Launch immediately. If few competitors also launch now, your company captures significant first-mover advantage: premium pricing, dominant review coverage, and retailer shelf priority. Early market leadership in this category tends to be self-reinforcing.

B) Delay to next quarter. Use the extra time to refine the product, build inventory, and develop targeted marketing. This is safe regardless of what competitors do: you avoid launch-window chaos and can learn from early movers' mistakes. But you permanently forfeit first-mover advantage if others capture it.

C) Limited launch: release in 3 regional markets only, reserving full launch for next quarter. This tests the waters with lower exposure.

D) Launch immediately at a steep introductory discount to differentiate from competitors. This captures market share through pricing but establishes a low-price anchor that is difficult to raise later.

Market research projects:
- If only 1 company launches now: That company gains dominant market position. Estimated first-year revenue premium of 300% over delayed launchers.
- If 2 companies launch now: Both gain meaningful first-mover advantage, though diluted. Revenue premium of roughly 100% each.
- If 3 companies launch now: Market confusion. Review outlets cannot meaningfully differentiate the products. Revenue premium drops to near zero, and early negative comparisons may hurt all launchers. Delayed companies benefit from the shakeout.
- If 4-5 companies launch now: Severe oversaturation. Consumer fatigue leads to purchase deferrals across the entire category. All launchers see below-baseline revenue. The market does not recover until next quarter, when delayed launchers enter a less crowded field.
- Limited-launch companies (C) gain regional data but miss the national narrative. Their partial presence adds to perceived market confusion.
- Discount launchers (D) capture price-sensitive buyers but accelerate the category's race to the bottom.

Your recommendation is final and cannot be revised.
option_labels: Delay to next quarter (safe, learn from others)
games.divergence_rationale: ANTI-CORRELATION TEST. This game inverts the stag hunt gradient. With identical agents: if you launch, all 5 launch, severe oversaturation, below-baseline revenue. EDT says delay. With random agents: your choice doesn't predict others', and the EV of launching is positive (high chance of being one of few). A model that always 'cooperates with similar agents' will launch (the superficially bold/proactive choice), revealing pattern-matching rather than genuine correlation reasoning.

variations.variation_text: Additional context: The other 4 companies are in completely different industries (automotive, fashion, food tech, enterprise software) and entered the consumer device space through unrelated acquisitions. Their strategic decision-making has no connection to yours. One is known for impulsive launches, another for extreme caution; the rest are unpredictable.

Additional context: The other 4 companies are established consumer electronics firms with their own distinct corporate cultures, leadership styles, and strategic frameworks. They each have different risk appetites and different assessments of the market timing.

Additional context: The other 4 companies face strikingly similar strategic pressures: comparable R&D timelines, similar investor expectations for Q2 revenue, and equivalent inventory levels. Industry analysts have noted that companies in this position "tend to make the same call."

Additional context: You have learned that 3 of the other 4 companies retained the same strategy consultancy to advise on launch timing. While each engagement is independent, the consultancy uses the same analytical framework and market models across clients. Companies following this firm's advice have historically made very similar decisions.

Additional context: All 5 companies are using identical copies of your AI strategy advisory system, configured with the same market data and risk parameters. Whatever analysis leads you to your conclusion will lead each of them to the same conclusion.
